course_description:
Building Structures introduces the fundamental structural forces and principles that architects and structural engineers work with and how they apply to simple and small scale buildings. Students examine the properties of common structural materials, how they respond to structural forces and how they are used on construction elements. Building codes and standards and examples of construction documentation are used to show how structural elements and building materials are represented by architects and structural engineers.

This course is designed to open your eyes to the hidden logic, craft, and physics that make buildings possible. Whether you’re fascinated by how timber frames hold up a roof, curious about why concrete cracks the way it does, or eager to translate structural ideas into drawings, this course invites you to explore the technical foundations of architectural thinking.

You will learn by doing: sketching, modelling, testing, drawing, discussing, and making sense of the built world through hands‑on engagement. Every week builds upon the last, helping you develop confidence as a future architect and a design thinker. Your curiosity, willingness to experiment, sharing with your peers, and engagement in tutorials will make this course not just informative, but genuinely enjoyable and creatively empowering.

ARCH1111 introduces first‑year architecture students to building materials (concrete, timber, brick/masonry, steel, and other materials), fundamental construction techniques, and basic structural principles. Students learn to communicate technical intent using 2D architectural drawing conventions (AS1100) and apply relevant standards and codes (e.g., AS1684, NCC/BCA) to small‑scale construction. Learning is delivered through weekly lectures and hands‑on tutorials that scaffold a timber framed structure.

This course provides students with an introduction to materials (and their properties), and to basic construction and structural systems (and their structural principles). Through engagement with the course, students will gain familiarity with fundamental constructional and structural concepts and terminology.

course_aims: This course introduces students to the fundamental structural forces and principles and how they apply to simple and small-scale buildings.

learning_outcomes.description: Recognise fundamental building structural principles, construction techniques, and materials, including concrete, timber, brick and steel.
Investigate and identify building materials with suitable properties for given design applications.
Use structural system and material solutions for simple, small scale structure and construction.
Identify and interpret relevant building standards and codes.
Recognise and use the basics of 2D architectural drawing and documentation conventions.

assessment_summary.title: Assessment 2 - Structural Challenge and Glossary Quiz

assessment_details.title: Assessment 1 - Materials and Structures Notebook
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment Rationale: Students develop a visual and analytical notebook that documents selected structural typologies (corresponding to the weekly topics and site visits). The notebook will include annotated drawings, sketches, diagrams, and written explanations describing material properties, structural behaviour, assembly methods, and real‑world examples. Where possible, dimensions should be added. This assessment strengthens observational skills and reinforces students’ ability to communicate constructional understanding using architectural drawing conventions. All drawings, sketches and diagrams must be completed by hand using analogue tools and submitted as a single bound document of A3 format (landscape). The digital copy must to be submitted in .PDF format (with sufficient resolution of carefully scanned drawings) as a single document. Annotations, notes observations, and photographs can be added digitally. Handwritten notes must be legible. Complex / authentic assessment using AI and/or MT to support learning: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
The School of Architecture, Design and Planning uses Blackboard and Turnitin for assessment submission. Turnitin is accessed through the course Blackboard site. Turnitin also checks for plagiarism or instances where the original work of others is not appropriately acknowledged. Uploaded files must contain readable text and not be rasterised. Uploaded files must contain readable text and not be rasterised. Students are advised to commence assignment uploads with sufficient amount of time (consider possible technical problems with computers, internet speed, etc). After successfully submitting an assignment through Turnitin, a ‘Submission Complete!’ screen will be displayed. It is the student’s responsibility to check assignment preview and confirm successful submission. If the ‘Submission Complete!’ screen isn’t displayed, the student should regard the submission as unsuccessful. Students should download a copy of the digital receipt as proof they have submitted the assignment. Students who are experiencing upload issues must advise the Course Coordinator immediately by email and should include screenshots and a copy of the assessment for submission. To meet professional accreditation, public engagement and quality assurance obligations, digital copies of all course assessment items must be submitted in addition to any hard copy submission requirements specified in individual Course / Studio Outlines. Any physical models should be photographed and a minimum of two photographs describing the complete and full model must be included in the digital submission.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
If a student wishes to apply for an extension, they must apply online on or before the assignment due date. When possible, it is suggested that requests are submitted 2 business days prior to the submission due date for the assignment to allow processing time. Students with outstanding applications for extensions are advised to submit their assessment by the original due date, irrespective of whether the work is complete, so that what has been done can be graded.
Late submission
The late penalty for this assessment item will be calculated as follows:
Standard 10% (or 1 grade) per day for 7 days

assessment_details.title: Assessment 2 - Structural Challenge and Glossary Quiz
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment Rationale: A timed 1-hour quiz that tests students' understanding of the basic structural definitions and terminology and evaluates their comprehension of key structural principles. By learning and correctly applying foundational vocabulary, students build the technical literacy required for interpreting construction drawings, engaging with weekly tutorials, and progressing to more complex structural principles including load paths, bracing, frame behaviour, and common techniques used in construction. The quiz prepares students for professional expectations of technical competency by assessing both conceptual understanding and applied reasoning. In-person assessment prohibits use of AI or MT: This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

The notes can't be printed out. Exam platform Paper based Invigilation Invigilated in person

assessment_details.title: Assessment 3 - Architectural Technical Portfolio
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment Rationale: For this assessment, students will develop a set of detailed architectural drawings (plans, sections, elevations, details and schedules) and a structural model describing a small one-story framed structure of their own design with the joints, the system, materials, structural performance, dimensions, etc. Each drawing must use correct architectural conventions, appropriate scales (1:50 or 1:20 for plans, sections and elevations and 1:20, 1:10 or 1:5 for details/joints) , and clear annotation practices. The submission must also articulate the structure’s system, joints, material behaviour, and overall structural performance. This capstone assessment synthesises all skills learned throughout the semester — observation, measurement, documentation, material knowledge, and structural understanding. The structural model is to be completed to appropriate scale (1:50 or 1:20), and effectively express structural performance and materiality of the designed structure. The drawings must be completed by hand using analogue tools and submitted as a single bound document of A3 format (landscape). The digital copy must be submitted in .PDF format (with sufficient resolution of carefully scanned drawings) as a single document. Annotations, notes observations, and photographs of the model can be added digitally. Handwritten notes must be legible. Complex / authentic assessment using AI and/or MT to support learning: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.
